spec_match.rationale: Exercises the exact relaxation the spec ships: createElement and querySelector with names the HTML parser already accepted (Latin accents, CJK, punctuation, emoji).

interactive.rationale: Sample buttons plus an editable text field drive a live createElement + scoped querySelector with green/red panels per call — a real probe, not a code card.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'pick or type a tag name with unicode or punctuation and see whether createElement and querySelector accept it' — the page delivers that exactly.

edge_cases.rationale: Includes the canonical fail case (`123-bad`) and unicode passes; never demonstrates the upper-bound failures the spec still preserves (control chars U+0000–U+001F, space inside the name, the empty string), so a visitor doesn't see where the line still is.

references.rationale: See-also section links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/6278918763708416 and the DOM namespaces spec.

design_system.rationale: All colours pull from CSS variables; pass/fail uses --accent-emerald / --accent-rose with --text-black foreground — AA-clean.

openQuestions.title: Show the still-rejected names
openQuestions.detail: The probe only includes names that the new rule accepts plus the historical leading-digit fail. Adding rows for names with a space, U+0000, the empty string, and a `<` would teach the boundary the spec still enforces and stop the page reading as 'literally anything works'.

openQuestions.title: Surface the localName / tagName casing rule
openQuestions.detail: The page logs `localName` but doesn't explain that HTML stays ASCII-lowercased while non-ASCII characters keep their original case. A small two-row 'name in / name out' table would teach this without adding controls.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the symmetry with createAttribute
openQuestions.detail: createElement is one side of the relaxation; document.createAttribute (and Element.toggleAttribute) also benefit. A small adjacent probe panel would cover the rest of the surface without needing a separate page.

summary: Tight interactive probe for createElement / querySelector. The biggest missing angle is the still-rejected boundary — without it, the page implies all characters now work, which is not quite true.
